skill.name: spec
skill.description: Guides specification-driven development by turning requirements into staged requirements, design, and task documents.
skill.summary: Create structured SDD specs with EARS acceptance criteria and task breakdowns.
skill.author: Clionegohan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, Windows SAM, and weak cryptography patterns, but the cited text is Markdown examples, EARS templates, and ordinary workflow wording. No malicious prompt injection or credential exfiltration was found; the meaningful risk is that the skill documents file generation and branch or PR workflow calls that can affect a repository when paired with those skills.

medium_findings.title: Repository Workflow Actions Require User Oversight
medium_findings.description: The skill describes generating files under specs/ and automatically invoking /branch and /pr workflows after user confirmation. This appears intended for specification management, but it can modify repository state or trigger connected tooling if those related skills perform git or PR operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly lists generated spec paths and states that /branch and /pr are invoked from this skill. The examples include confirmation prompts, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Shell Execution Findings Are Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown code fences around diagrams, templates, and examples. I found no evidence that the skill contains executable Ruby, shell commands, or dynamic command construction at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each cited backtick area is a Markdown fenced block in SKILL.md. The surrounding content is documentation, not executable code.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM Findings Are EARS Template Text
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM detections occur in EARS acceptance-criteria placeholders containing the phrase THE SYSTEM SHALL. This is requirement-template language and not a reference to the Windows SAM database.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are template placeholders for specification writing. No Windows file path, registry hive, credential dump, or SAM database handling appears in the file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Wording Collisions
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections are false positives from words such as description, SDD, and Design Exploration. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, encryption code, or cryptographic configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe workflow phases or metadata. They do not define cryptographic algorithms or process secrets.

content.value_statement: Teams often turn vague feature requests into implementation work too early. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through requirements, design, and task breakdowns before files are produced.
actual_capabilities: Guides a three-phase SDD workflow for requirements, design, and task breakdowns.
Turns natural language requests into user stories and acceptance criteria.
Uses EARS-style templates for event, state, and conditional requirements.
Plans EPIC, Story, and Subtask specification files under a specs directory.
Prompts for user confirmation before phase progression and file generation.
Coordinates with branch and PR workflows described in related skills.
limitations: It provides workflow instructions and templates, not a standalone parser or validator.
Generated specs still require product and engineering review.
Branch and PR behavior depends on separate related skills being available.
The source instructions are primarily written in Japanese.

use_cases.title: Plan a new product feature
use_cases.description: Convert an early feature idea into requirements, design notes, and implementation tasks.

use_cases.title: Prepare engineering handoff
use_cases.description: Create clear acceptance criteria and task boundaries before development begins.

use_cases.title: Standardize spec documents
use_cases.description: Keep EPIC, Story, and Subtask files consistent across a repository.

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic spec
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin requirements discovery for a simple feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a spec for a user profile editing feature. Ask questions before moving to design.

prompt_templates.title: Add acceptance criteria
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn a feature request into structured EARS criteria.
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft requirements for password reset using EARS acceptance criteria. Include common failure cases.

prompt_templates.title: Explore technical design
prompt_templates.scenario: Move from approved requirements into design options.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the spec workflow for audit logging. Compare two design approaches and explain tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Create full task breakdown
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a complete EPIC, Story, and Subtask breakdown for multi-factor authentication. Confirm before file generation.

output_examples.input: Create a spec for OAuth login with multi-factor authentication.
output_examples.output: A staged requirements summary with personas, user goals, EARS acceptance criteria, design considerations, and a request for approval before continuing.

output_examples.input: Break this approved design into implementation tasks.
output_examples.output: An EPIC, related Stories, and focused Subtasks with dependencies, test expectations, and planned spec file names.

output_examples.input: Review the generated requirements for missing edge cases.
output_examples.output: A concise list of missing conditions, security considerations, and revised acceptance criteria for user approval.

best_practices: Approve each workflow phase only after reviewing the requirements or design output.
Keep each Subtask focused on one implementable behavior or capability.
Review generated file paths before allowing repository changes.
anti_patterns: Do not skip requirements confirmation when the feature scope is unclear.
Do not use vague acceptance criteria when EARS criteria would remove ambiguity.
Do not generate branch or PR changes without reviewing the proposed spec files.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates structured specification guidance for requirements, design, and implementation task breakdowns.

faq.question: Does it write application code?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on upstream specification work before implementation begins.

faq.question: What is EARS?
faq.answer: EARS is a structured format for clear acceptance criteria using trigger, condition, and expected behavior statements.

faq.question: Can it create files?
faq.answer: The workflow describes generating specification files after user confirmation.

faq.question: Does it require other skills?
faq.answer: The core spec workflow stands alone, but branch and PR steps depend on related skills.

faq.question: Is it safe for shared repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with review gates enabled, because it can plan repository files and related branch or PR actions.
